questions.q: What is the primary purpose of using delta time in a game loop?
options: To measure the total elapsed time since game start
To make movement and animation independent of frame rate
To synchronize audio with visual frames
To calculate the number of physics updates per second
questions.explanation: Delta time (time since last frame) allows frame-rate-independent movement because it scales all physics and animation by how much real time has passed, ensuring consistent behavior whether running at 30 or 120 FPS. Using total elapsed time would cause animation speed to depend on game age rather than real-world seconds.

questions.q: In a typical game loop, what is the correct order of operations?
options: Process Input → Update Logic → Render
Render → Process Input → Update Logic
Update Logic → Render → Process Input
Process Input → Render → Update Logic
questions.explanation: The standard order is Input → Update → Render because this allows the current frame to respond to user input accumulated since the last frame, then render the result. Processing input before updating ensures responsiveness without rendering stale data.

questions.q: What problem does a fixed timestep solve in game physics?
options: It reduces memory usage for physics calculations
It ensures physics simulations are deterministic and stable across different frame rates
It eliminates the need for collision detection
It allows rendering without waiting for physics to complete
questions.explanation: Fixed timestep runs physics at a constant rate (e.g., 60 Hz) regardless of frame rate, preventing instability where fast frames cause tunneling and slow frames skip collisions. Deterministic physics is essential for replays, networking, and consistency.

questions.q: A game is running at 144 FPS but needs physics at 60 Hz. What does the game loop do with the extra time?
options: Skip physics updates to maintain frame rate
Accumulate time and run multiple physics steps per frame when accumulated time exceeds timestep
Run physics at 144 Hz to match the render frame rate
Drop rendering frames until physics catches up
questions.explanation: Time accumulation lets variable render rates drive a fixed physics step: if 3 frames pass (1/48s) before physics timestep (1/60s) accumulates, one physics step runs. This decouples rendering from physics without losing accuracy or frames.

questions.q: Why might a game use vsync synchronization in its game loop?
options: To increase the game's frame rate beyond the monitor's refresh rate
To prevent screen tearing by aligning frame updates with the monitor's refresh cycle
To reduce input latency by processing input faster
To eliminate the need for delta time calculations
questions.explanation: Vsync stalls rendering until the monitor is ready for the next frame, preventing torn frames where old and new pixels appear on screen simultaneously. The trade-off is increased latency since the CPU must wait for the display.

questions.q: What does frame pacing accomplish in modern game loops?
options: It ensures the GPU finishes rendering before the CPU starts the next update
It caps frame rate to a target value to provide consistent frame timing and reduce power consumption
It synchronizes animation playback across multiplayer clients
It balances load between physics and rendering threads
questions.explanation: Frame pacing intentionally limits FPS (e.g., target 60 on mobile) to provide steady, predictable timing and reduce battery drain. Without pacing, uncapped rendering wastes power on frames the display can't show.

questions.q: In a game with a 16ms target frame time (60 FPS), if an update takes 20ms, what visible effect occurs?
options: The frame is rendered twice to compensate
Input is processed twice to catch up
The frame rate drops to 50 FPS for that frame
Physics calculations are skipped to return to 60 FPS
questions.explanation: If one iteration takes 20ms instead of 16ms, that single frame runs at ~50 FPS. Subsequent frames return to 60 FPS if the delay was temporary. Continuous overruns cause sustained frame rate drops.

questions.q: Why do some games decouple rendering frame rate from physics frame rate?
options: Physics at fixed rate is more stable; graphics can render at higher variable rate for smoothness
Rendering is faster than physics, so physics must run first
Physics simulation is visually distracting and should run less often
The CPU cannot handle both operations in the same frame
questions.explanation: Physics benefits from fixed timestep for stability and determinism, while graphics can render at higher variable rates (e.g., 60 physics, 144 rendering) to interpolate between physics states, providing smooth visuals with stable simulation.

questions.q: What is frame interpolation used for in decoupled render/physics loops?
options: To reduce the number of physics calculations
To estimate object positions between physics timesteps for smooth rendering
To synchronize animation speed across different computers
To prevent GPU memory overflow
questions.explanation: Between physics updates, previous and current frame object positions are blended based on elapsed time to create smooth motion for rendering, even though the underlying physics updates in discrete steps. This avoids the jerky appearance of rendering at physics rate.

questions.q: A multiplayer game wants deterministic physics for all players. Which loop structure best supports this?
options: Variable timestep physics synced to network tick rate
Fixed timestep physics run at a constant rate, with network messages timestamped to match
Asynchronous physics updates processed whenever the network receives new data
Physics running twice as fast as the network update rate to ensure all collisions are detected
questions.explanation: Fixed timestep ensures all players run identical physics calculations given identical inputs, making state predictable and easy to verify. Variable timestep causes divergence and cheating vulnerabilities since slow/fast clients get different results.
